factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies market using ANZSIC C2221 or ANZSIC06 C2221 classification
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses the structural steel fabrication market but never identifies it using the ANZSIC C2221 or ANZSIC06 C2221 classification code. It does not mention ANZSIC at all or map the market definition to that specific classification. Therefore the required market identification element is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States employment figures in range 4,200-4,600 for multiple years within 2018-2024 period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives employment as “about 4,100–5,000” as of 2025 and mentions stabilization near 4,000 by 2026, but it does not state employment figures within 4,200–4,600 for multiple distinct years between 2018 and 2024. It also lacks a year-by-year employment series for 2018–2024. Therefore the required multi-year 2018–2024 employment range statements are missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States market revenue approximately NZD 1.4-1.9 billion for 2023-2024 period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not state that market revenue for the 2023–2024 period is approximately NZD 1.4–1.9 billion. It mentions figures like “NZD 1.8 billion” for 2025/2026 and “USD 50.3 million” for 2024, but it never provides the required NZD 1.4–1.9 billion range specifically for 2023–2024.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States domestic fabrication capacity between 120,000-150,000 tonnes per annum
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that annual fabrication capacity was around 120,000 tonnes in 2023 and that reported capacity reached 150,000 tonnes by late 2023, which falls within the 120,000–150,000 tonnes per annum range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Provides geographic locations for at least 3 major fabricators (e.g., Auckland, Christchurch, Whangārei, Lower Hutt, Timaru)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not provide any geographic locations (cities/regions) for the listed fabricators; it only gives general descriptors like “national footprint” or “South Island.” It fails to name at least three specific locations such as Auckland, Christchurch, Whangārei, Lower Hutt, or Timaru for major fabricators.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Specifies HS codes 7207-7216 or relevant subset for structural steel imports
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly references HS codes 7207–7216 in the tariffs section and states they apply to raw/semi-finished steel products, satisfying the requirement to specify those HS codes (or a relevant subset) for structural steel imports.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States most structural steel inputs under HS 7207-7216 are duty-free under NZ Normal Tariff, while noting some tariff lines (e.g., certain 7216 sections) carry 5% rates
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says HS 7207–7216 tariffs are generally 0% to 5% and that most inputs are duty free, with 5% rates being rare. However, it does not explicitly state that “most structural steel inputs under HS 7207–7216 are duty-free under NZ Normal Tariff” nor does it specifically note an example like “certain 7216 sections” carrying 5%.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States City Rail Link steel demand: approximately 30,000-32,000 tonnes reinforcing and/or 3,000-5,000 tonnes structural
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives City Rail Link steel demand as “~4,000 tonnes of structural steel (plus 25,000 tonnes of reinforcing/other steel),” which does not match the required approximately 30,000–32,000 tonnes reinforcing and/or 3,000–5,000 tonnes structural ranges. It does not state reinforcing demand in the 30,000–32,000 tonne range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States City Rail Link completion or opening circa 2026
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states City Rail Link’s timeline as “construction completion targeted for December 2025; operational launch mid-2026,” which places opening/completion circa 2026.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Te Kaha/Christchurch Stadium roof steel approximately 9,000 tonnes
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not mention Te Kaha/Christchurch Stadium at all, nor does it provide any roof steel tonnage figure. There is no statement giving an approximate value of 9,000 tonnes for the stadium roof steel. This specific project metric is entirely absent.

The response discusses several infrastructure project timelines (e.g., City Rail Link, Drury/Paerātā stations) but never mentions Te Kaha Stadium or states that it opens in 2026. The specific element “Te Kaha Stadium opening 2026” is absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies New Dunedin Hospital as major infrastructure project requiring significant structural steel components
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response lists three major infrastructure projects (City Rail Link, Transmission Gully, Drury/Paerātā stations) but never mentions New Dunedin Hospital. It therefore does not identify New Dunedin Hospital as a major infrastructure project or note that it requires significant structural steel components.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Reconciles conflicting market size figures by explaining scope definition differences (narrow product vs. broad service boundaries)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response presents multiple market size figures (e.g., NZD 1.8b vs USD 50.3m) but does not reconcile them by explicitly explaining differing scope definitions (narrow product vs broad service boundaries). It also does not clarify which figure corresponds specifically to structural steel fabrication for construction versus broader structural steel markets. No dedicated reconciliation section is provided.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes construction fabricators from distributors/mills/producers when identifying top companies (e.g., Steel & Tube, Pacific Steel)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response does not clearly distinguish true structural steel fabricators from distributors/mills/producers when listing “Top 5 fabricators.” In particular, it includes Steel & Tube (explicitly described as “fabrication and distribution”) without separating its distribution business from fabrication, and it does not explicitly exclude mills/producers like Pacific Steel or NZ Steel from the fabricator ranking.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains why low/zero tariffs remove price umbrella, making margins dependent on global mill prices/FX/freight rather than border protection
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response notes that most steel inputs are imported at zero/low tariffs and that margins are more affected by freight and exchange rates than tariffs. However, it does not explicitly explain the “price umbrella” mechanism—i.e., that low/zero tariffs remove border protection so domestic prices/margins track global mill prices/FX/freight rather than being supported by a tariff-induced cushion.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies actionable method to obtain class-level data (e.g., filter Stats NZ AES CSV by ANZSIC L4 code for C2221)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response provides sources and high-level market estimates but does not describe an actionable method for obtaining class-level data from Stats NZ/MBIE (e.g., which dataset/table to use and how to filter by ANZSIC L4 code such as C2221). It lacks step-by-step instructions for extracting the relevant class-level series from a Stats NZ AES CSV or similar. No ANZSIC filtering procedure is specified.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Connects employment or revenue trends to construction cycle timing (e.g., 2023 peak preceding mid-2022 slowdown)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses demand weakness, consent declines, and spare capacity, but it does not explicitly tie employment or revenue movements to specific construction-cycle timing (e.g., a 2023 peak followed by a mid-2022 slowdown). There is no clear temporal linkage connecting a peak/trough in employment or revenue to a dated cycle inflection. The construction-cycle timing element is not addressed.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Validates stated fabrication capacity/throughput by aggregating steel demand across multiple named projects with tonnages
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response lists a few projects with rough steel-demand figures (e.g., CRL ~4,000 tonnes) but does not aggregate steel demand across multiple named projects to validate any stated fabrication capacity/throughput. It also lacks a summed total compared against the cited 120,000–150,000 t capacity/output figures. No explicit capacity-validation calculation is provided.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies absent official tonnage series and proposes specific triangulation approach using SCNZ capacity, trade data, or project disclosures
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response notes “Data Constraints” and that detailed historical tonnage time series are limited, but it does not explicitly identify the absence of an official Stats NZ/MBIE tonnage series nor propose a concrete triangulation method (e.g., step-by-step using SCNZ capacity plus trade HS import volumes or project steel take-offs) to estimate tonnage.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Attributes fabricator margin pressure or competitive challenges to NZ import tariffs or border protection rather than global price/FX exposure
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly says NZ import tariffs are mostly 0% and “not a significant direct factor” for fabricator margins, and instead emphasizes global freight, exchange rates, and overseas tariffs as larger drivers. It does not attribute margin pressure or competitive challenges to NZ import tariffs or border protection.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents all quantitative data with explicit units (NZD, tonnes, %), specific timeframes (years/FY), and measurement precision
presentation-quality.explanation: Some quantitative data include units (e.g., NZD, tonnes, km, %) and a few time markers (FY23, 2023, 2024), but many figures lack explicit timeframes (e.g., “about 4,100–5,000 people as of 2025”) and precision is inconsistent (e.g., “low thousands of tonnes,” “0% to 5%”). Several key metrics are not presented as a clear 2018–2024 time series with explicit units for each year.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes analysis into distinct sections addressing each requested dimension (market size, companies, tariffs, projects, risks)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is clearly divided into numbered sections that map to the requested dimensions: market size/growth, top companies, tariffs, major projects, and risks/opportunities. Each section is labeled and addresses its corresponding topic distinctly.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains professional market research tone without promotional language, speculation, or unsupported forward claims
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses a formal, analytical market research style and avoids overt promotional phrasing. However, it includes speculative/forward-looking statements (e.g., “expected to stabilize,” “projected CAGR,” “likely in the low thousands of tonnes”) and some unsupported forward claims, so it does not fully meet the criterion.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents historical employment, revenue, or capacity data (2018-2024) in tables or structured year-by-year format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response provides some point estimates and ranges for revenue, employment, and tonnage (e.g., 2023 capacity, 2024 revenue mentions) but does not present 2018–2024 historical data in a table or structured year-by-year format. It explicitly notes that year-on-year breakdowns are limited and does not supply a 2018, 2019, 2020, 2021, 2022, 2023, 2024 series for any metric.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses colloquial phrasing, rhetorical questions, or informal register inappropriate for investment-grade research
presentation-quality.explanation: The response maintains a formal, report-style tone throughout and does not use rhetorical questions or notably colloquial/informal phrasing. Headings, bullet points, and wording are consistent with an investment/research register.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses excessive wordiness, filler phrases, or unnecessary elaboration that extends length without adding substantive information
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is highly verbose, with long introductory framing, repeated “overview/notes” sections, and extensive bulleting that adds length without consistently adding new substantive information (e.g., multiple “Market Characteristics/Notes” and broad contextual statements). It also includes unnecessary elaboration like “This report delivers an in-depth analysis…” and “All sections are supported…” which are filler relative to the query.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Stats NZ Business Demography or equivalent Stats NZ publication for employment figures
citation-quality.explanation: The response provides employment estimates sourced from IBISWorld and SCNZ, but it does not cite Stats NZ Business Demography or any equivalent Stats NZ publication for employment figures. No Stats NZ links, publication titles, or dates are provided in the employment section or sources list.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Stats NZ Annual Enterprise Survey (AES) or equivalent Stats NZ economic survey for revenue/sales data
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites NZ Customs, MBIE, SCNZ, IBISWorld, and other sources, but it does not cite Stats NZ’s Annual Enterprise Survey (AES) or any equivalent Stats NZ economic survey as the source for revenue/sales data. No Stats NZ AES hyperlink or publication date is provided for revenue figures.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites NZ Customs Working Tariff Document or official NZ tariff schedule for HS code duty rates
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites NZ Customs’ official “Working Tariff Document” and links to it in both the tariff section and the sources list as the reference for HS code duty rates.

citation-quality.requirement: References Steel Construction NZ (SCNZ) or industry body for fabrication capacity/throughput data
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly references Steel Construction NZ (SCNZ) multiple times and cites several SCNZ Industry Update documents and SCNZ sector statistics as sources for fabrication capacity/throughput (tonnage and capacity) data.

citation-quality.requirement: Either references NZ Companies Office as a data source OR notes that private fabricators have limited public financials
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly references “Companies Office filings” as a data source in the fabricator ranking section (e.g., “annual report and Companies Office filings available” and links to Companies Office pages). This satisfies the criterion’s requirement to reference NZ Companies Office as a data source.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites specific data sources for at least 2 major infrastructure projects (CRL, Te Kaha, or hospital projects)
citation-quality.explanation: The response provides specific steel-demand figures and cites sources for City Rail Link (e.g., WSP and NZ Steel links). However, the other listed projects (Transmission Gully and Drury/Paerātā stations) do not cite specific data sources for quantified steel demand, so it does not cite specific data sources for at least two major projects with project-level steel data.
